reason: Prompt clause is jammed into every sentence with nonsensical evidence stubs like "By around 1850 mattering," plus stock meta asides that read as template assembly rather than a timed student LEQ.
Evidence tokens ("The Election around 1944 mattering," "Soon around 1883 mattering") are period-wrong placeholders, and the same classmate/teacher filler lines appear as generator chrome.
Opening pastes the full prompt as a broken clause, then uses "Alger Hiss mattering" and bolted-on "Hard to finish in time" rather than organic unfinished writing.
Repeated "Demographic around 1701 mattering" / "Navigation Acts mattering" stubs and truncated prompt paste ("North…") are composition artifacts, not exam prose.
Nearly empty body recycles the prompt verbatim three times with only "From around 1775 mattering" as fake evidence and identical worksheet/teacher stock lines.
Formulaic body paragraphs name events as "X mattering" while repeatedly embedding the full prompt phrase; evidence (Townshend/Quartering) sits outside the 1607–1754 frame in a non-student way.
"Disputes With Japan Meanwhile around 1940 mattering" and triple "Hard to finish in time" inserts are mechanical timing theater, not a real clock-pressure essay.
Uses Neutrality Acts (1930s) as "mattering" evidence on a 1945–1991 Cold War prompt and ends with the same cross-shard classmate/worksheet boilerplate.
Evidence set (First Continental Congress, Revenue Act 1764) is chronologically off for 1500–1763 Native/European conflict and is glued on via the shared "X mattering" template.
"Amendment Jacqueline Kennedy Voting mattering" is unreal nonsense; "Hard to finish in time" appears three times as pasted filler rather than authentic truncation.
